author: GeoSot

body:
Since I'm the one to blame for the implementation, I wanted to put in my two cents.

With regards to the overwriting behavior questioned here https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/issues/37245#issuecomment-1269040410, this was in line with the prior spec:
https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/blob/088c727a31fd5a700f1d0024fef834a1720c9692/js/tests/unit/dom/data.spec.js#L30-L44

---
About this PR. The fix is breaking the expectation imho (if i'm not mistaken) e.g.:

```js
const instance1 = new Component(element);
const instance2 = new Component(element);

Component.getInstance(element) === instance1 // true
Component.getInstance(element) === instance2 // false
```

This could be considered a breaking change.

The second instance won't be set into the registry but it is still being initialized. As such, if someone is expecting `getInstance()` to return the actual instance for the element, this won't be the case.

---

My first proposal for the implementation was _throwing_ an error but it was decided against doing it that way, in risk of breaking people's code (https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/pull/32180#discussion_r531000255). On a side note - even with the fix or with the implementation that is in the main branch, there is the possibility to instantiate various components on the same element, even though only one is stored in the registry.

If we really would like to enforce some certain behavior, i'd rather propose to throw an error. That way the instantiation of another component would be cancelled, instead of just showing a message and preventing the registry to be updated.

Changing the PR to throw an error could be considered a breaking change, and won't really fix the issue mentioned in #37245. So another approach could be to `dispose()` a component if it already exists in the registry, prior updating it - instead of requiring a user to do it manually. Imho this should be a proper way of dealing with that issue.

@GeoSot @XhmikosR WDYT?

---

Update:

Pushed a draft PR with the solution mentioned here - see #37584

Another approach could be to always return an instance saved in the registry if someone tries to reinitialize a component that already was bound on an element. This could eventually render the `getOrCreateInstance()` method obsolete, as that would make it the default behavior. Not sure though how to deal with updating the config in that case. Any thoughts?

FWIW in a current project, banner isn't in source files but only in dist files.

body: I am also currently wondering if adding `focusable="false"` to the `<svg>` elements could be useful since it may cause an accessibility issue in Internet Explorer?
author: MewenLeHo

body:
`title` technically provides an accessible *description*, rather than an accessible *name*.

maybe a slicker way around it is to add `aria-label` to the `<button>` - then you can save yourself the `aria-hidden="true"` on the internal svg as well, since the `aria-label` on the parent will completely replace/override whatever's inside the button anyway?

``` html
<button ... aria-label="Try" title="Try it on StackBlitz">
  <svg class="bi"><use xlink:href="#lightning-charge-fill"/></svg>
</button>
```

as for `focusable="false"`, not sure if we still need to cater to IE since it's now been officially deprecated by Microsoft, and that was the only browser exhibiting this odd behaviour.
author: patrickhlauke

body:
also, note that

> Svgs inside 'Try it on StackBlitz' and 'Copy to clipboard' buttons are pure decoration so they must be ignored by any assistive technology.

is perhaps not completely accurate. They're not pure decoration, they represent the content/label of the button... but that's probably splitting hairs

> ```

Ok with your version and for your opinion about `focusable="false"` since IE is not listed anymore in the list of supported browsers on [.browserslistrc file](https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/blob/v5.2.2/.browserslistrc)

However I think it will be better to keep the same content in the two attributes:

> ```
> <button ... aria-label="Try it on StackBlitz" title="Try it on StackBlitz">
>   <svg class="bi"><use xlink:href="#lightning-charge-fill"/></svg>
> </button>
> ```
author: MewenLeHo

body: depending on browser/assistive technology used, both the accessible name and description may be announced, resulting in an overly verbose announcement (though, at least with Chrome/NVDA and Chrome/Jaws, it seems that if both are exactly the same, it's only announced once). Will test some more later just to check...
author: patrickhlauke

body:
> `title` technically provides an accessible _description_, rather than an accessible _name_.

I've actually just tested it, and it seems that consistently browsers use/expose `title` as an accessible *name* if it's the only thing present, rather than using it as a *description*

Long story short, after more testing (also in Firefox/Win and Safari/macOS), ignore my suggestion. This will be fine as is ;)
author: patrickhlauke

body: also, i'd note that those buttons were accessible even before this, but they did lead to an unnecessarily verbose announcement (along the lines of "image Try it, button, Try it on StackBlitz"). so this PR is more of a nice tweak ("Try it on StackBlitz, button")

body: personally, i quite liked the headings inside the callouts ... makes it more easily skimmable (you can find what you're looking for specifically, AND it gives a hint about what the note is about and whether or not you can skip reading it altogether). so i'm a bit ambivalent here (particularly since skipping heading levels is not necessarily a WCAG failure  https://github.com/w3c/wcag/issues/655)
author: patrickhlauke

body:
> personally, i quite liked the headings inside the callouts ... makes it more easily skimmable (you can find what you're looking for specifically, AND it gives a hint about what the note is about and whether or not you can skip reading it altogether). so i'm a bit ambivalent here (particularly since skipping heading levels is not necessarily a WCAG failure [w3c/wcag#655](https://github.com/w3c/wcag/issues/655))

Yes, of course you're right, that it is not an accessibility requirement, just a good practice. However, I also find a bit strange to see callouts headings in the table of content of the page (wether they are at the good level or not), for example: 

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/16681352/207379392-83e79bfd-6b73-4cb6-8ea7-0f2c06531606.png)

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/16681352/207370499-708985e8-14f9-4d96-9f49-5fb913e7adbc.png)

The headings HTML tags could be replaced by the use of a class like `.h4` or `.fw-bolder`. This would also harmonize the appearance of all callouts which currently do not all have the same level of heading.

body: Agreed with headings too. If a11y is an issue, we should look into using `fw` or `hN` classes maybe?

they do head up distinct sections of content, so personally don't think they're strange ... just a bit more specific than the very generic headings for the standard sections.

Open to more iterations here, but for now going to ship and iterate :).

body:
@mdo made some futher tweaks, particularly by adding `overflow: overlay` which at least normalises how Chrome/Windows positions the scrollbar - it looks the same, but is not inside the content part, so bottom padding applies the same way to Chrome/Windows and other browsers with the thinner overlay scrollbar. To clarify, these are for historical reasons mostly, used for the old docs, pre v4.0.

We should not touch them, because they are unrelated to the current state of things.

Also, generally, try to avoid touching anything in gh-pages without having the accompanied changes in the main branch. That is, if we want to add/change redirects for the current docs, we should have them in the main branch first.
author: XhmikosR

body: Understand the arguments even if having https://getbootstrap.com/examples/blog/ redirecting to 3.4 instead of 5.3 bothers me. If someone thinks it is useful to start something in the _main_ branch to add redirections, please let me know.
author: julien-deramond

body:
We shouldn't touch such ancients redirects. We should try not to break anything out there :)

We can start making root redirects for some of our current pages, but it's an extra thing to keep in mind when updating the gh-pages branch in case these redirects are removed in the future, so we need to think about the approach.
author: XhmikosR

body: It's not that important right now no problem. But just FYI we did it in Boosted via https://github.com/Orange-OpenSource/Orange-Boosted-Bootstrap/pull/1088. It is mainly extra work when you deploy the HTML files in the `gh-pages` because you have a lot of more dirs to handle containing `index.html`. Works well btw but pretty boring to deploy. That's the limit of static redirections with Hugo; at least what we succeeded to do.
author: julien-deramond

body:
I don't mind the number of files; I mind that I have to remember to delete the folders manually, which is error prone.

That being said, I'm not against having more root redirects, assuming we need them and use them.

We can use Cloudflare too, but I'd rather keep most redirects here, unless we need some wildcard ones like we did with alpha domains bulk redirects.
author: XhmikosR

body:
The big advantage was for us to have a "latest" URL and keep almost-always-valid URLs with:
- /docs redirecting to /docs/5.2/getting-started/introduction
- /docs/getting-started to /docs/5.2/getting-started/introduction
- /docs/getting-started/introduction to /docs/5.2/getting-started/introduction

Same thing for all URLs and "sub-URLs"

body: That we can do, the current old redirects can stay. But again, it's too troublesome to update, so I'd like this to be handled by Hugo ideally. Otherwise, we need a maintainable way for this.

If you'd rather skip all updates until the next major or minor version, let me know by commenting `@dependabot ignore this major version` or `@dependabot ignore this minor version`. You can also ignore all major, minor, or patch releases for a dependency by adding an [`ignore` condition](https://docs.github.com/en/code-security/supply-chain-security/configuration-options-for-dependency-updates#ignore) with the desired `update_types` to your config file.

If you change your mind, just re-open this PR and I'll resolve any conflicts on it.

body:
> Thanks for this PR @astagi. We haven't looked at it yet but could you please remove the modifications in `dist/js`? It's going to be generated/built when we'll make the release.

Sure @julien-deramond just removed and forced update.

@XhmikosR do you mean `bootstrap.esm.js`? I think is not needed, that's why I removed it from build scripts. The remaining structure will be

dist
- js

author: astagi

body:
I don't think we need another JS dist file. For package users, using src/index.esm.js should be enough. Have you tried it with specify bootstrap/js/src/index.esm.js?

When the ESM files were introduced, we only targeted browser users and we shouldn't introduce any breaking changes.
author: XhmikosR

body:
> I don't think we need another JS dist file. For package users, using src/index.esm.js should be enough. Have you tried it with specify bootstrap/js/src/index.esm.js?
> 
> When the ESM files were introduced, we only targeted browser users and we shouldn't introduce any breaking changes.

@XhmikosR haven't tried using src/index.esm.js directly, I usually compile everything using Rollup and its plugins and put generated files and maps in the dist folder. I agree with you, in this case it's the same files structure you have in src.. I don't know if using src/index.esm.js directly is the correct way, in that case src/index.esm.js file should be referenced in package.json as module alongside the sideEffects (this property actually activates tree shaking).

I'm curious to try modules in browsers, I usually bundle everything in a single js files using modules from packages, anyway I don't think things would change: imho using a single esm file with class declarations and code to initialize components can't be optimized in any way.
author: astagi

body: @XhmikosR another thing that concerns me: using src/index.esm.js directly as a module would skip all the Rollup plugins chain such as Babel.. any idea on this?
author: astagi
astagi

body:
@XhmikosR @julien-deramond I've just force updated this branch, I made some tests and as @XhmikosR suggested, using only `"js/index.esm.js"` as module everything works as expected ([Luxon library uses this approach as well](https://github.com/moment/luxon/blob/master/package.json#L61)). Now we have

- `dist/bootstrap.esm.js` (and minified version) for browser usage.
- `js/index.esm.js` as module for Webpack, Rollup etc.. with `sideEffect` set to false to enable tree shaking.

[Here](https://github.com/astagi/bs-treeshaking-issue) you can find my demo updated with this configuration to see things in action! Same results, no breaking changes, no file touched/added in dist folder 🎉
author: astagi

body:
Unfortunately, it is still a breaking change because the now referenced file is not passed through babel.

That's why I only decided to include the js/index.esm.js for people to manually reference it when they build Bootstrap. For browser-ready builds only the dist file can be used like before.

We need to revisit our bundles in v6 and see what we are shipping. And also, remove the dist files from the repo and later see if we can split our CSS and JS into separate packages.
author: XhmikosR

body:
> Unfortunately, it is still a breaking change because the now referenced file is not passed through babel.
> 
> That's why I only decided to include the js/index.esm.js for people to manually reference it when they build Bootstrap. For browser-ready builds only the dist file can be used like before.
> 
> We need to revisit our bundles in v6 and see what we are shipping. And also, remove the dist files from the repo and later see if we can split our CSS and JS into separate packages.

Sorry @XhmikosR what do you mean with "the now referenced file"? Index.esm.js? It doesn't need to be passed through Babel, is something that should be used with bundlers like Rollup or Webpack and is up to users to make it pass through Babel for the final bundle in their project configuration.. this is the same approach used by Luxon and it's the correct way.. the old bootstrap.esm.js that passes through Babel is the file that can be used in browsers, if you reference this file inside module it won't be tree shaked and this will impact on the final bundle size..

Let me also add that I don't know how many users are using that module directly in browsers, I followed the documentation to use Bootstrap with Rollup.js using import syntax, that's the only thing I found about modules and following the documentation I discovered that tree shaking was missing.
author: astagi
astagi

body: The change in package.json is the breaking change. Before the file was passed through Babel, now it isn't.
author: XhmikosR

body:
> The change in package.json is the breaking change. Before the file was passed through Babel, now it isn't.

@XhmikosR Yes I know. With my modification index.esm.js is not passed through Babel because it's intended to be used as module by users.. users can do *import {Button} from "bootstrap" * in their projects and then use it as a module (parsing with Babel using their specific configuration, bundle it and minify). The module section in package.json is not intended to be used by browsers but by Node.js, imho there's no need to make it pass through Babel (it will be done later by the user in their projects if they need).

On the other side bootstrap.esm.js (that is still present and not touched by this modification) is intended to be used by browsers, and people must define a pathmap or include it directly to use it inside browsers (and this happened before my modification as well) despite the file defined in package.json.

Please see Luxon source code linked above. Sorry for these long posts and thanks for your patience and your replies, but I don't understand what's wrong with this PR. I'd like to understand to improve bootstrap-italia project as well.

body: Now `&::-moz-color-swatch` and `&::-webkit-color-swatch` are the same and could be deduplicated into one rule, to save a few characters.
author: alecpl

body:
> Now `&::-moz-color-swatch` and `&::-webkit-color-swatch` are the same and could be deduplicated into one rule, to save a few characters.

I don't think so. CSS specs states that if a user agent doesn't recognize part of a selector, it has to ignore the whole selector and its block. Namely, since one vendor's browser doesn't understand other vendors' prefixes, it has to drop any rules that contain those unrecognized prefixes.

Firefox is notorious for partially flouting this rule. Therefore it could work on this particular browser but non on Chromium based ones.

That's why Botstrap is keeping distinct rules for different vendors' prefixes, like in `_form-control.scss` or `_form-range.scss` for instance.

body:
IMO we can let it as it is now since it's just a matter of styling; both are possible (source: https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTML/Element/kbd).
If others think this PR brings more clarity too, I don't mind making such a change but the PR will need to modify all occurrencies in the project where `<kbd>` is used.
author: julien-deramond

body:
Hi @julien-deramond, thank you for your comment.
You're right, it is eventually a matter of style. However, the difference between Bootstrap kbd style and the one in the MDN documentation, is the fact that in the MDN documentation, all the examples containing nesting have custome styling in their CSS to support it using CSS combinators, in a way that makes the output more understandable. On the other hand, in bootstrap, I believe the KBD tag does not support nesting. That is why thee "+" was styled as a Keyboard input. We can either remove the nesting, or we can also add custom styles for nesting in the CSS. I guess we can also disregard the pull request as at the end of the story it is just a matter of style.
Thank you for your input!
author: ChellyAhmed

body:
I guess then we can drop the nesting approach (as suggested in this PR but everywhere in the docs): `<kbd><kbd>Key</kbd> + <kbd>Other Key</kbd></kbd>` → `<kbd>Key</kbd> + <kbd>Other Key</kbd>` for better readability.

On my side I haven't found any evidence of a better a11y with the nesting approach.

Before applying this modification everywhere, @patrickhlauke do you have more info about whether it's best to surround the combination of keys with a `<kbd>` for a11y purpose?
author: julien-deramond

body:
for what it's worth, screen readers don't make any special announcement about `<kbd>` being there or not. these sorts of text level semantics generally just don't get announced in any special way. so purely from a screen reader experience perspective, there's no material difference (heck, there's no difference to whether you use `<kbd>` or not in the first place).

so I think it comes down to personal preference. personally, i'm ok having the plus sign all part of the `<kbd>` as `<kbd>` isn't about the individual *key*, but about the keyboard input you need to perform, and i doubt (though might be wrong) that people get confused about the fairly common case of "something plus something else" meaning that they need to press these simultaneously (rather than needing to press the `+` key)
author: patrickhlauke

body:
Hi @patrickhlauke , thank you for your input.
It might be unnecessary to clarify it, especially for experienced people. But I guess it may be helpful for those who are not familiar with Bootstrap. For instance, if we go back to the MDN Docs shared above, even in the nested case, there is always a difference between the keys to be pressed, and the indicative "+" sign.
I also think it won't be difficult to go through all the KBD tags and remove the nesting in all the documentation.

body:
I've updated it to:

> The hide method will complete asynchronously (assuming animations are enabled). As such, calling the `dispose` method immediately after `hide()` will lead to incorrect results.
> 
> Here's an example of the proper usage:
> 
> ```js
> const myModal = document.querySelector('#myModal')
> myModal.addEventListener('hidden.bs.modal', event => {
>   myModal.dispose()
> })
> myModal.hide()
> ```

---

The example code still isn't ideal - eg I was wondering if the event listener ought to include `{ once: true }` - but that's probably dependant on the rest of the user's code.

(It's kind of an awkward API, seems a shame there's not something built in like `myModal.hide().then(m => m.dispose())`...)

I can add a incorrect example like @julien-deramond suggests, but IMHO it's over-complicating it - let me know which you'd prefer.

body: TBH I'm not really convinced for now by the need to have a counterclockwise direction animation for the spinner.
author: julien-deramond

body:
That's understandable, and there may be cases where a counterclockwise direction animation for a spinner is not necessary or appropriate. Ultimately, the design of the spinner should be driven by the specific needs of the website or application where it will be used.

However, there may be some cases where a counterclockwise direction animation could be beneficial. For example, if the spinner is being used to indicate a process that is running backwards or to undo an action, a counterclockwise direction could be more intuitive for users. Additionally, for accessibility purposes, counterclockwise motion is often more comfortable and natural for left-handed users.

Ultimately, the decision to use a counterclockwise direction animation for a spinner should be based on the needs of the specific use case and the preferences of the designer or developer. If the designer or developer feels that a counterclockwise animation could be beneficial or add value to the design, it may be worth considering.

body:
Just leaving it here, but might be linked to #36277. 

As mentionned in the options, it seems like the only `fallbackPlacements` we support (https://main--twbs-bootstrap.netlify.app/docs/5.3/components/popovers/#options) are `top right bottom left`, am I right @GeoSot ?

FYI, the same padding everywhere was done on purpose because of a rendering issue. (https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/issues/36069#issuecomment-1116951571).
Both PRs introduce some visual regression centering the tooltip while scrolling.
author: louismaximepiton

body:
> As mentionned in the options, it seems like the only `fallbackPlacements` we support (https://main--twbs-bootstrap.netlify.app/docs/5.3/components/popovers/#options) are `top right bottom left`, am I right @GeoSot ?

It seems that all variations should also be supported, because the `fallbackPlacements` documentation redirects to the [Popper documentation](https://popper.js.org/docs/v2/modifiers/flip/#fallbackplacements) and Popper introduce all variations as possible (see https://popper.js.org/docs/v1/#popperplacements--codeenumcode). I don't see any mention that indicates that only `top right bottom left` placements are supported.

> Define fallback placements by providing a list of placements in array (in order of preference). For more information refer to Popper’s [behavior docs](https://popper.js.org/docs/v2/modifiers/flip/#fallbackplacements).

By the way, just look this piece of code:

https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/blob/80ba84844399bd3e83ed77d42202ea8e7a3620d4/scss/_tooltip.scss#L97-L110

The selectors are like `[data-popper-placement^="top"]` and not like `[data-popper-placement="top"]`. The character `^` is added on purpose to take into account all values that are prefixed with `top`: in our case, this is done to take into account all variations (`top`, `top-start`, `top-end`).

- [Changes of the old PR #36277](https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/pull/36277/files): it created the body placement issue with placement variations and that's why we are making this PR now.
- [Changes of this current PR #38121](https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/pull/38121/files): it's just a rollback of https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/pull/36277.

body:
@XhmikosR Could you give me any news for this kind of feature please? Because on mobile devices, this issue causes huge ergonomic problems in some cases.

Scenario example:

1. The user uses an Android mobile device.
2. The DOM has an `.offcanvas` with `position: absolute`.
3. The `.offcanvas` contains an `<input type="text">`.
4. The user click on the `<input type="text">` to write something.
5. The `.offcanvas`  will be hidden because its position is not `fixed`. Explanation:
    - Clicking on the `<input type="text">` will open the mobile virtual keyboard.
    - When opening the mobile virtual keyboard, the `resize` event will be triggered on the `window`.
    - Because of this event, the current listener will be executed.

This is a very annoying issue that I can't solve in any other way. And I'd need a way to disable this behavior.

body:
Another thing that is pretty problematic and that will maybe force us to land this PR in v6 only is that when `$theme-colors-dark` primary color is modified to be green for example in dark mode, buttons are not modified because they are defined with a loop over `$theme-colors`. This loop should use custom properties instead, but doing that will break the compilation because of what we're doing in `button-variant()` mixin with the use of `tint-color`, `shade-color`.

As discussed, @louismaximepiton, I let you create a branch based on this one to check what would be the necessary changes in order to make the buttons dependent on `$theme-colors-dark` as well. From that, we'll decide if this PR could be merged or not in v5.

body:
Hi Patrick,

I'm trying to achieve a [side navigation](https://deploy-preview-1349--boosted.netlify.app/docs/components/side-navigation/#collapsible-with-drawer-content) (heavily draft) using the tab js. It works well until I change the role from `tablist` to `menubar` and `tab` to `menuitem`. Then if I focus the tab element (left part of the example) and I play with arrows as mentionned in the description, I can't reaccess the tab element when I drop the focus on it.
author: louismaximepiton

body:
> I'm trying to achieve a [side navigation](https://deploy-preview-1349--boosted.netlify.app/docs/components/side-navigation/#collapsible-with-drawer-content) (heavily draft) using the tab js

I'm not 100% convinced that particular UI widget is best suited to be represented as a tabbed interface, rather than a set of accordions/menus/megamenus ?

do you have a working "after" example that shows the behaviour or your specific case/example with your changes applied?

unless i'm mistaken, you're basically trying to hijack the tabs behaviour for non-tabs, and bumping against the guardrails that try to make sure this code only runs on a tabbed interface? looking over the code change, you'll achieve the effect visually, but the currently selected/expanded/live "menuitem"/"tab" also won't programmatically expose that it's expanded/open, which is doesn't sound good from an accessibility point of view...
author: patrickhlauke

body:
> I'm not 100% convinced that particular UI widget is best suited to be represented as a tabbed interface, rather than a set of accordions/menus/megamenus ?

I was trying to achieve it only using the Bootstrap bundle but yeah it's far from ideal right now. Haven't thought about using the megamenus since those weren't in Bootstrap bundle but yeah I could try to implement it like that 🤔 
 
> do you have a working "after" example that shows the behaviour or your specific case/example with your changes applied?

Not yet but I could maybe give it a try.
 
> unless i'm mistaken, you're basically trying to hijack the tabs behaviour for non-tabs, and bumping against the guardrails that try to make sure this code only runs on a tabbed interface? looking over the code change, you'll achieve the effect visually, but the currently selected/expanded/live "menuitem"/"tab" also won't programmatically expose that it's expanded/open, which is doesn't sound good from an accessibility point of view...

Yes you're right, I'm trying to hack a bit the component here and it doesn't update well the aria attributes.

The main issue for me here that it seems we allow people to use several ways to make a working tab:
```js
const SELECTOR_TAB_PANEL = '.list-group, .nav, [role="tablist"]'
const SELECTOR_INNER = `.nav-link${NOT_SELECTOR_DROPDOWN_TOGGLE}, .list-group-item${NOT_SELECTOR_DROPDOWN_TOGGLE}, [role="tab"]${NOT_SELECTOR_DROPDOWN_TOGGLE}`
```

But only one way keep the behavior so I was a bit surprised.

I can see two ways to resolve this issue:
* Changing/Removing the role should at least not impact the behavior : the way I do in this PR (or maybe a better version of what's done atm), but we leave folks the possibility to have a not well accessible tab element. IMO, could be done until v6.
* Changing/Removing the role should impact the behavior : Change the selectors might be a better way to achieve this but it might be breaking for folks and maybe we should wait for v6 ?

body:
Thanks for spiking something out like this, but to save your time and effort next time, please wait for some discussion before jumping into developing a new feature :). We don't want to waste time or discourage folks. In this case, this isn't the right way to add a new component, and I don't see us adding something that native browsers give us for datetime inputs.

Thanks though!
author: mdo

body:
>  In this case, this isn't the right way to add a new component, and I don't see us adding something that native browsers give us for datetime inputs.


Perhaps bootstrap can provide a way to accept folders of custom extension functions, so that some users can access their favorite functions and integrate into bootstrap more smoothly and conveniently.
Of course, there may be better ways to do this, and the main reason people are willing to incorporate functionality into bootstrap is that they can be better and more comprehensively maintained.

body:
> TBH, I don't really like having the new version number of Bootstrap mentioned since it means that we'd need to change it afterwards, meaning more maintenance for future.

Yep, I agree with it. Tried to match the existing pattern in v3 and v4 but I can change it everywhere to use the exact same label without mentioning the version. Other thoughts in the team?
author: julien-deramond

body:
Converted the PR to draft so that we discuss it first.

I think we should go with with the same way for all old banners; no need to mention the version. And maybe don't touch v3; we need to be extra careful there because the HTML files were minified.

Either way, thanks for tackling this, we'll land one solution or another. :)
author: XhmikosR

author: mdo

body:
v3: It can't be reviewed so easily was my point, since the files were minified.

As for the rest, I still don't think we should tie things to a specific version number. The message should read: There's a newer major version of Bootstrap. Or something like that :)
author: XhmikosR

body: Oh I'm down with that actually.
author: mdo

body:
In this PR, we might take the opportunity to make this banner accessible by adding a visible focus.

I was thinking about how to improve the workflow after this PR. It could be interesting to add in our _main_ branch at the right place the code for this banner associated with a `.d-none` class so it is not displayed while it is the latest version. But when we will release a new version, by going back to `gh-pages`, we would only have to remove this class to render it correctly. I'm thinking about it because it is easier to change the rendering in the _main_ branch with the right classes rather than after when the classes might have changed.
author: julien-deramond

body:
We shouldn't **really** try to improve the old version docs. They are a snapshot of what we had on the development branch when the version was current.

Just add the link to the main site with a general message and we are done with it. We won't have to go back to them again for the same issue.

As for the future, we only need to do this on main and it should be discussed then. Generally, adding useless stuff just in case we need it, it's not preferable, at least to my eyes; when we need something we add it then.
author: XhmikosR

body: BTW, I'm not saying I'm against your idea for the next versions, we just need to discuss it in a PR that implements it (CC me).
author: XhmikosR

body:
> We shouldn't really try to improve the old version docs. They are a snapshot of what we had on the development branch when the version was current.

I'm not talking about improving the old version docs. I'm only talking about adding a class or two in the existing banners to make them accessible since I have to change their labels as well. If not possible with classes, let's forget it.
I understand your point about the docs in `gh-pages` being snapshots of what we had on the development branch when the version was current, and I agree. But that's not true for these banners that have been added in `gh-pages` after.

For the new banners, I have to choose a style with classes. So while I'm at it, if possible, let's use classes that allow the visible focus to be displayed.

> As for the future, we only need to do this on main and it should be discussed then.

Yep, that's what I explained. Just wanted to suggest this idea in this context. I'll create a PR then to tackle this specific topic. No problem.

author: julien-deramond

body:
> Thanks for the PR @crespire, there are some issues to fix with the content that are in my comments.
> 
> I'm wondering if it's not a duplicate of https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/getting-started/javascript/#events. This concept is already explained generally in the JavaScript documentation.

Thanks for the note. I'm happy to change this to a link reference instead if you feel that's more ideal. I think sometimes folks read sections of docs in isolation and details like this can get lost or can be a somewhat "unknown unknown" for more junior folks (like myself!).

Let me know what direction you want to take on this and I'm happy to make the changes, but I've accepted your other suggestions!
author: crespire

body:
What do you think if instead of the code example, we describe the event more precisely by referencing this use case like that:

```diff
diff --git a/site/content/docs/5.3/components/modal.md b/site/content/docs/5.3/components/modal.md
index 9f42f5aba..25b7f3a3a 100644
--- a/site/content/docs/5.3/components/modal.md
+++ b/site/content/docs/5.3/components/modal.md
@@ -856,7 +856,7 @@ Bootstrap's modal class exposes a few events for hooking into modal functionalit
 {{< bs-table >}}
 | Event | Description |
 | --- | --- |
-| `hide.bs.modal` | This event is fired immediately when the `hide` instance method has been called. |
+| `hide.bs.modal` | This event is fired immediately when the `hide` instance method has been called. Can be prevented by calling `event.preventDefault()`. See [JavaScript events documentation]({{< docsref "/getting-started/javascript#events" >}}) for more details on event prevention. |
 | `hidden.bs.modal` | This event is fired when the modal has finished being hidden from the user (will wait for CSS transitions to complete). |
 | `hidePrevented.bs.modal` | This event is fired when the modal is shown, its backdrop is `static` and a click outside of the modal is performed. The event is also fired when the escape key is pressed and the `keyboard` option is set to `false`. |
 | `show.bs.modal` | This event fires immediately when the `show` instance method is called. If caused by a click, the clicked element is available as the `relatedTarget` property of the event. |
```

It would keep the documentation DRY by referencing the general events documentation and make the hide prevention capability more discoverable.
author: julien-deramond

>  | `show.bs.modal` | This event fires immediately when the `show` instance method is called. If caused by a click, the clicked element is available as the `relatedTarget` property of the event. |
> ```

I think that's a good idea! I'm not tied to my proposed changes.

author: XhmikosR

body:
> That being said, you are welcome to propose a clean approach with the least changes and we can discuss it there. #34989 needs to close because is abandoned.

Well, I was trying to revive #34989 . 

> Secondly, the lint approach does not cover all the cases last time I had a look.

Does it need to? I guess we don't really need to cover 100% of the cases, just make it so bootstrap can be used on pre-rendering contexts like Angular's or vue-prerender. An headless browser will not click on buttons and expand menus, it just needs to load the page, save the HTML and move on. 

With that said, the problem isn't that objects such as `document` are required but that Bootstrap will call them right away on load. I understand why it does it but for pre-rendering purposes a simple way of telling Bootstrap to NOT register any even listeners or run additional code would be enough.

body:
Thanks for opening a PR @romankupchak93 
Haven't checked the content of the PR but first things first.

As mentioned in our [Contributing Guidelines](https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/blob/main/.github/CONTRIBUTING.md#pull-requests):

> Do not edit bootstrap.css or bootstrap.js, and do not commit any dist files (dist/ or js/dist). Those files are automatically generated by our build tools. You should edit the source files in [/bootstrap/scss/](https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/tree/main/scss) and/or [/bootstrap/js/src/](https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/tree/main/js/src) instead.

Please update your PR.

Please also use the PR description with a detailed explanation of your changes.

body: @mukesh-lt the thing with this approach is that tests become more unpredictable. We don't know which device is used explicitly beforehand. Are these changes really needed in browsers.js?
author: XhmikosR

body:
> @mukesh-lt the thing with this approach is that tests become more unpredictable. We don't know which device is used explicitly beforehand. Are these changes really needed in browsers.js?

author: hannahiss

body:
> I would propose to add label's id as the first value, since using `aria-labelledby` for mandatory information could be a good idea...?

While I understand the "could be a good idea" part, I personally think this ends up being massively overengineered for what it actually is/does (particularly for a generic example in our documentation). It's been generally accepted practice to use `aria-describedby` to link additional info and instructions for a form field. The nuance of "if this is mandatory, it should be in the accessible label, not in the accessible description" is one that I'd say should be left up to authors to decide. Any more information on the original assertion that `aria-labelledby` is better supported than `aria-describedby`?

body:
Thanks for this PR @Ionaru 

While testing your modification (which seems to be an improvement), I'm wondering if there's not also another bug.

`storedTheme` is only set from the local storage when the script/page is loaded, and is never changed again. So there are use cases where one can play with the OS light/dark/auto appearance, and the color displayed in the documentation is the opposite.

Example of use case that doesn't work:
1. Choose OS appearance as dark, load the website and choose "Auto", reload the page
1. Change OS appearance to light → website is light 🟢 
1. Change OS appearance to dark → website is dark 🟢 
1. In the dropdown, choose "Dark" → website stays in dark 🟢 
1. Change OS appearance to light → website is light 🔴 

I'm wondering if we shouldn't do something like that (or transform `storedTheme` as a function that gets the value from the local storage):

```diff
diff --git a/site/static/docs/5.3/assets/js/color-modes.js b/site/static/docs/5.3/assets/js/color-modes.js
index 4528ba36b..c8bdf7d6f 100644
--- a/site/static/docs/5.3/assets/js/color-modes.js
+++ b/site/static/docs/5.3/assets/js/color-modes.js
@@ -7,11 +7,9 @@
 (() => {
   'use strict'
 
-  const storedTheme = localStorage.getItem('theme')
-
   const getPreferredTheme = () => {
-    if (storedTheme) {
-      return storedTheme
+    if (localStorage.getItem('theme')) {
+      return localStorage.getItem('theme')
     }
 
     return window.matchMedia('(prefers-color-scheme: dark)').matches ? 'dark' : 'light'
@@ -56,7 +54,7 @@
   }
 
   window.matchMedia('(prefers-color-scheme: dark)').addEventListener('change', () => {
-    if (storedTheme !== 'light' || storedTheme !== 'dark') {
+    if (localStorage.getItem('theme') !== 'light' && localStorage.getItem('theme') !== 'dark') {
       setTheme(getPreferredTheme())
     }
   })
```

/CC @mdo
author: julien-deramond

body:
@julien-deramond You're completely right. I also detected that it wasn't perfect but couldn't nail it down, with your steps I was able to reproduce the issue.

While I'm personally not a fan of repeating `localStorage.getItem('theme')`, I also realise this snippet is just an example for developers to implement their own color-scheme-switchers. However it will probably be copy-pasted a lot. 😅
So I think it's best to wrap that in a function.

I've pushed an update with your suggestions implemented.
author: Ionaru

body:
> While I'm personally not a fan of repeating localStorage.getItem('theme'), I also realize this snippet is just an example for developers to implement their own color-scheme-switchers. However, it will probably be copy-pasted a lot. 😅
> So I think it's best to wrap that in a function.

Yep definitely, repeating `localStorage.getItem('theme')` wasn't elegant at all 😇 . It was mostly when I tested the use cases just to be 100% sure of the value. The function version is way better!

Regarding your other comment, I completely agree. This snippet could stay as-is and not be such an issue for a first version because not that blocking and it'll rarely occur.
But the important part is that, yeah, it'll be copy-pasted and probably never updated later by the majority. This is why I'd like to ship this fix for the v5.3.0 if that's still possible.

Waiting for other thoughts from the rest of the core team.

author: jynsy

body:
It's not really related to the bundlewatch action even if the content would be almost the same to prepare the environment.
As a first step, I think you could make your modification in a separate isolated `.yml`. And we'll discuss later on with the rest of the core team (except if they have some inputs right now) if we try to merge your `.yml` with one of the other `.yml`s to maybe save some resources (not run `npm run dist` 2 times).

On my side, I was thinking of something like this:
1. Checkout PR branch
2. Set up Node.js
3. Install npm dependencies with `npm ci`
4. Run dist with `npm run dist`

_(so until this 4th step, it's, as you saw, basically the same thing as `bundlewatch.yml`)_

5. Compare `dist` content to _main_ branch dist content
   a. If the content is the same: job's green
   b. If the content is different: it's an error, and if possible the diff is displayed in the console in the corresponding action so we don't have to run it locally
   
What do you think?
author: julien-deramond

body:
Ok, I think I got it.

1. I will create a separate file with same preparations of the environment
2. Then compare dist contents of both main branch and dependabot branch
3. If they are the same the job is successful
4. If they are different the job should fail and display diff

As it stands, I killed about a day last year trying to figure out why Bootstrap 5.3 broke all my popovers. One way or another, I'd like to save others from that pain.

author: adriweb

body:
This topic has been discussed outside of GitHub. In summary, we have mixed feelings because this was an undocumented functionality that some users rely on due to the permissive nature of the interface, leading to an unintended regression when we refactored some aspects. On the other hand, within our JavaScript philosophy, providing internal access to a component is not considered good practice.

Given these factors, the most favorable course of action is to merge this PR for version 5, as it will address the regression for those relying on it. However, we may reconsider or modify this approach in version 6. This could involve changing the JavaScript interface or better documenting these types of usages.

author: julien-deramond

body: @julien-deramond You didn't appreciate my effort. It seems good, doesn't it? As a beginner, don't I have the right to share my ideas?
author: ibrataha8

body:
> As a beginner, don't I have the right to share my ideas?

Yes, you are very welcome to share your ideas, and everyone is.

For your next PRs in this repository, please fill out the "Description" and "Motivation" fields in the PR. Otherwise, we can't know what are the justifications for your changes, and what are your ideas.

> It seems good, doesn't it?

IMHO it doesn't feel good since by adding emojis for each title in the README file, you are also modifying the anchors URLs; for example `README.md#bootstrap-5` becomes `README.md#bootstrap-5-🅱%EF%B8%8F` and so potentially breaks already used URLs by folks.

Still IMHO, I believe that having titles without emojis remains more accessible.

The rest of the core team is also notified, so if they think this PR brings some value they can reopen it.
author: julien-deramond

body: @julien-deramond  I only seek to rectify what I can, with the help of God's guidance. Any mistakes are mine, and any influence from the devil. I seek refuge in God from forgetting Him and strive to be a bridge that leads you to paradise, not a means to be thrown into the fire.

We tried something via https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/pull/39132 but fixing `divide()` by handling the dividend unit doesn't seem compatible with `node-sass` we still maintain (see https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/pull/39132#issuecomment-1704705053).

I suggest that we merge + ship this patch. And then, if we get rid of the `node-sass` compatibility in v6, go with a solution based on what's in https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/pull/39132.

It means that there would be nothing to do for now in `twbs/rfs`.

@twbs/css-review all fine with this proposal? Thoughts?

@xeron I'm not sure what you you're referring to as confusing; I tested on mobile and it seems to work as expected. This patch doesn't affect hover state - it only adds the missing active state (matching the active state of regular buttons).

Edit: it's true that the hover state doesn't have the shade that it does on regular buttons, but my understanding is that this has always been the case and is the intended design for outline buttons. I think a change to the hover color would require a separate discussion and merge request.
author: theodorejb
xeron

body:
> What's confusing on mobile is after clicking the button it stays highlighted because of the sticky hover effect

I see. Note that regular buttons have this sticky-hover behavior on mobile too, so it's not something specific to outline buttons (nor is it affected by this pull request).

My goal with this patch is to specifically address the missing active state on outline buttons, and not change anything else. As you said the sticky-hover effect may warrant a separate design discussion.

body: I added a second commit which updates the outline button hover color to match that of regular buttons. Previously the hover state was the same as the non-hover state of regular buttons, which could cause confusion when hovering an outline button placed next to a non-outline button. This also improves the active state appearance since it no longer looks too dark in comparison to the hover color.

body:
One problem with this callout is that if a user were to do as it says and copy the code above the callout:

```html
<link href="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/bootstrap@5.3.1/dist/css/bootstrap.min.css" rel="stylesheet" integrity="sha384-yvuw7Qka5HyIDWlQLof1Eh7TsntO8T9n5xVQ2fLEK8UEb47oC0LAx6N9KUadRsT8" crossorigin="anonymous">
<script src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/bootstrap@5.3.1/dist/js/bootstrap.bundle.min.js" integrity="sha384-AN46EzEu8aYMuLb9XLkZgyvOCua6xS1HKstfL9xcOyx+UKZYcp2Z58pZjezzj/Qc" crossorigin="anonymous"></script>
```
...And replace `https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/` with `https://unpkg.com/` then the links wouldn't work because the SRI appears to be different.

Quick test: https://stackblitz.com/edit/bootstrap-cdn-test?file=index.html

Maybe link to this page as an alternative CDN instead: https://cdnjs.com/libraries/bootstrap ?
author: coliff

body:
Yeah @coliff the integrity value must be changed to `<link rel="stylesheet" href="https://unpkg.com/bootstrap@5.3.1/dist/css/bootstrap.min.css" integrity="sha384-4bw+/aepP/YC94hEpVNVgiZdgIC5+VKNBQNGCHeKRQN+PtmoHDEXuppvnDJzQIu9" crossorigin="anonymous">` for example for this file. Integrity value can be generated via https://www.srihash.org/ but it's not really user-friendly I suppose (and missing in my warning callout message).

Might be worth pointing to https://cdnjs.com/libraries/bootstrap instead as you said which offers a "Copy Script Tag".

Changing back this PR to draft mode and I'll do the modification later on.

body:
Hi there, FWIW the current code will always apply `inherit`, singe the last wins, in CSS — so whatever your tool hints, at least the order must remain the same.

No need for the webkit prefix either, Autoprefixer will take care of it if needed.

IMO the code should be:

```css
text-align: inherit;
text-align: match-parent;
```

It'd need to be checked through BrowserStack I guess, because the `inherit` fallback could be useless now, depending on our target (needs to check Safari 12, after reading current browserslist configuration file).

body:
Hello @julien-deramond ,

form what I've learned (but I could have missed something) labels (after `.form-control`) inside a `.floating-label` are displayed as labels when (https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/blob/413894d4691b5ebb1377fcf5ab8fd3bba49766a8/scss/forms/_floating-labels.scss#L54)
- the `.form-control` has focus (`.form-control:focus`)
- the control is a `.form-select`
- there is no placeholder attribute (`.form-control:not(:placeholder-shown)`)
- the control is a `.form-control-plaintext` (btw, I use this for divs when displaying application entity properties) 

In all the cases listed above the label is actually positioned as an inside-input label and assigned a different color and size (more appropriate for the label role), while the default behavior (https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/blob/413894d4691b5ebb1377fcf5ab8fd3bba49766a8/scss/forms/_floating-labels.scss#L12) is to take the place of the input placeholder.

Granted, at the same time it also acts as a label, so it has to be easy to read (like a placeholder). What is kind of confusing is that the `.form-floating input`s look much like already filled inputs rather than inputs with placeholders.

Thanks and kind regards!

body:
Hi @Muskan-Rao 
Thanks for submitting a PR to this repository :)

Our "templates" are called examples in this repo and are available in `site/assets/content/docs/5.3/examples` (in the source code) and at this URL https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/examples/.
They all follow the same architecture in this directory.

I've run your project locally and it is assembling and adapting to a real use case with different parts already available (not exactly the same but in the same spirit) in our examples such as [Jumbotron](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/examples/jumbotrons/), [Pricing](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/examples/pricing/), [Features](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/examples/features/), etc.

So this is not something we can include in our examples.

However, if not already the case, I'd suggest you to keep this project somewhere by creating for instance your own repository within your GitHub space where it could be helpful for other Bootstrap users, or be used as a portfolio, etc.

I'm going to close this PR as it can't be integrated as is in this repo, but keep up the good work 💪

body:
I'd say this is a good thing to make it customizable for consistency with other "Sass/CSS interfaces".

However, I'm not sure it should be rounded by default in terms of usage. Most of the time, they are used as headers, so it could cause some trouble by default like we can see in basically all our examples containing a navbar as a header (e.g. https://deploy-preview-39326--twbs-bootstrap.netlify.app/docs/5.3/examples/album/).

Maybe we can do the same thing as for the breadcrumbs where `$breadcrumb-border-radius: null !default;` but is still customizable via:
* `$breadcrumb-border-radius`
* `--#{$prefix}breadcrumb-border-radius`
* or by using `.rounded-*` utilities

@twbs/css-review thoughts?

_Note: tagging it for v5.4 for now_

body:
Hi @louismaximepiton,

The trade-off to the increase in size is to enable [`postcss-rtlcss`](https://github.com/elchininet/postcss-rtlcss) to process the css and generate a single, optimized file with selectors prefixed with `[dir=rtl]` and `[dir=ltr]`. `postcss-rtlcss` is a plugin promoted by the reputable module bundler plugin [`postcss`](https://github.com/postcss/postcss), however `postcss-rtlcss` does not process `/* rtl:options */` directive or any of its derivatives.

The changes proposed enable the plugin to process the css file while functionally maintaining the [`rtlcss`](https://rtlcss.com/) output.

Cheers,

body:
This beats the point of using the SVG sprite, though. And makes the situation even worse for maintenance, see #39809.

We should streamline our icons usage and generally not try to fix StackBlitz if we are going to duplicate stuff and make maintenance harder.
author: XhmikosR

body:
> This beats the point of using the SVG sprite, though.

Yes, considering the overall architecture of the documentation: the SVG sprite is better. However, it's probably less understandable to the end-user point of view for these examples in terms of autonomy and reusability (copy/pasting the code).

> And makes the situation even worse for maintenance, see #39809.

I get your point about maintenance. For these use cases, maybe we don't necessary need these icons to be exactly the same as the final version of the ones in Bootstrap icons. Or maybe in Hugo we can make some of them reusable with partials (or anything else).

> We should streamline our icons usage and generally not try to fix StackBlitz if we are going to duplicate stuff and make maintenance harder.

There's something to consider regarding the usage of the documentation by the users; the ease to understand, integrate into their projects and to create reproducible use cases when they create issues. I'm not saying I'm against your points, just that everything needs to be taken into account, not only docs' maintenance.

Since I don't have a strong opinion on the topic and I just would like these StackBlitz examples to be fixed, I let you decide with @mdo and @louismaximepiton which version would be better between this one and https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/pull/39505, or any other strategies.

Note that if this one is closed, there's probably something to update in our Alerts documentation, because it's basically what's already done there.
author: julien-deramond

body:
It's one thing trying to make docs better and another make it harder for maintainers to maintain the docs.

The icons at this point are a mess. We have numerous icons everywhere, with no easy way to see what's used and what's not.

So, I'd rather have a few broken icons at the moment or just drop the StackBlitz examples from those than make the situation worse right now.

We need to figure out a simpler way to handle the icons before scattering more icons across the docs. body:
The idea of the PR was mainly to be a quick fix: to have the same focus visible rendering for the nav links, the `.navbar-brand` and the skip links.

This fix only works for Chrome and Firefox.
Safari was not handled because the `.navbar-brand` and the skip links have already a contrast issue; the outline not being handled the same way for Safari.

![2024-03-23 08 04 25](https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/assets/17381666/560a66fe-e0e6-4573-b7bc-f31c7a73aec3)

We could tweak the whole rendering obviously for multi-browsers consistency, but it would take more time.

body: I'm just not sure the blue color along with white is good. Can't we just use white? Or will not look good in some cases?
author: XhmikosR

body:
> I'm just not sure the blue color along with white is good. Can't we just use white? Or will not look good in some cases?

I think we could fine-tune the outline to be a white one-line (maybe with a bigger width) instead of two in the documentation header only, including skip links, since the background colors are known and with a dark tint both in light and dark modes.

Or we could do something more generic in the doc, like what's done in our Boosted documentation: a general rule that uses a double outline (black and white) that works everywhere and doesn't need fine-tuning: see https://boosted.orange.com/. With black and white double-outline for security, we're pretty sure that one color will be contrasted enough whatever the context. It's maybe less nice visually but works well overall.

body:
Seems great! Thanks for the contribution.

The third point makes me wonder: could we move `rtl:remove` and `rtl:ignore` directives to their block version, to ease the move?

This shouldn't have any impact on our side.
author: ffoodd

body:
Hi @ffoodd,

>The third point makes me wonder: could we move rtl:remove and rtl:ignore directives to their block version, to ease the move?

What do you mean moving them to their block version?

I have plans of making this transparent for clients. As the intention of the RTLCSS directive is the same (do not create an RTL counterpart in the new stylesheet), what I want to achieve is that if someone uses a `remove` directive, this is translated to an `ignore` directive behind the scenes. If this is implemented in some moment, it is possible to remove the third point in the `Important things to take into account` callout warning.

But just let me know if you think that I should change that point or remove something from it at the moment.

Regards
author: elchininet

body:
My point is that moving directives as recommendend in your third point can be easily achieved un Bootstrap.

If we do it, would it ease your plugin usage?
author: ffoodd

body:
>My point is that moving directives as recommendend in your third point can be easily achieved un Bootstrap.
>If we do it, would it ease your plugin usage?

Ah, got it. I would not change anything in the Bootstrap source code because `rtl:ignore` directives also exist on RTLCSS and they have a different functionality than the `rtl:remove` ones. At the moment Bootstrap uses the `rtl:remove` directives to avoid including specific rules into the `*.rtl.css` bundles. If they are replaced with `rtl:ignore` ones, those rules will be included in the `*.rtl.css` bundles but without flipping and I think that is not the intention.

body: Ah, got it too. Thanks, that's clearer 👌

body:
I don't have time to dig more into this right now, but there's also a possibility that our current version is correct (means that this PR should be closed) and that instead of setting `$modal-inner-padding` to `0`, it must be set to `0px` when using it like https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/issues/39798#issuecomment-2040301613:

```scss
$modal-inner-padding:  0px !default; // stylelint-disable-line length-zero-no-unit
```

Without unit added to `0`, it's confusing for the browser to handle the calculation between a unitless value and a value. Like it would be an issue with `min()` and `max()` functions. See https://drafts.csswg.org/css-values-4/#comp-func where the spec mentions:

> For all three functions, the argument [calculations](https://drafts.csswg.org/css-values-4/#calc-calculation) can resolve to any [<number>](https://drafts.csswg.org/css-values-4/#number-value), [<dimension>](https://drafts.csswg.org/css-values-4/#typedef-dimension), or [<percentage>](https://drafts.csswg.org/css-values-4/#percentage-value), but must have a [consistent type](https://drafts.csswg.org/css-values-4/#css-consistent-type) or else the function is invalid; the result’s type will be the consistent type.

And https://drafts.csswg.org/css-values-4/#css-consistent-type:

> Two or more calculations have a consistent type if [adding the types](https://drafts.css-houdini.org/css-typed-om-1/#cssnumericvalue-add-two-types) doesn’t result in failure. The [consistent type](https://drafts.csswg.org/css-values-4/#css-consistent-type) is the result of the type addition.

I'll try to find more time to check that more in detail.

body:
Superseded by #42544 for v6. The underlying problem here — closing the panel scrolls the page back to the toggler, breaking anchor links inside it — is resolved in v6's Drawer by restoring focus with `{ preventScroll: true }` (focus still returns to the trigger for accessibility, but the page no longer jumps). v6 also renamed Offcanvas to Drawer, so this patch to `offcanvas.js` no longer applies.

The configurable opt-out this PR adds (`scrollAfterClose` / `focusAfterClose`) was not carried over — if you still want an API to skip focus-restore entirely, please open a fresh feature request against v6-dev.

Closing.

author: mdo

body:
Apologies for letting this go stale. I believe that I first encountered this with Bootstrap v5.33 and SASS v1.77.8 Unfortunately it is quite a bit of work to replicate an environment entirely.

The SASS documentation describes a difference between quoted and unquoted strings. I am quite certain that all of the instances in this pull should be quoted strings, not unquoted strings. The SASS documentation says:
> Unquoted strings are written as CSS [identifiers](https://drafts.csswg.org/css-syntax-3/#ident-token-diagram)

> it’s generally a good idea to write quoted strings unless you’re specifically writing the value of a CSS property that uses unquoted strings.

https://sass-lang.com/documentation/values/strings/

Additionally, searching the Bootstrap repo for the `map-get` function shows that there is an inconsistency across the repo with using quoted or unquoted strings with `map-get` (this pull fixes this).
https://github.com/search?q=repo%3Atwbs%2Fbootstrap%20map-get&type=code

**So, regardless of which environment reproduces it, it seems that some judgment should be made on which is correct (quoted or unquoted strings) to make all instances consistent.**

I have now rebased to latest `main` and double-checked that this pull still fixes all of the relevant lines.

author: patrickhlauke

body:
Just for info, after some discussions with the accessibility team, they confirmed that aria-hidden is needed on SVGs until now, even if most of assistive technologies will ignore SVGs that do not contain texts. They rely on criterion [WCAG 1.1.1](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/Understanding/non-text-content.html) saying:

> Non-text Content (Level A)
> Success Criterion (SC)
> All [non-text content](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/Understanding/non-text-content.html#dfn-non-text-content) that is presented to the user has a [text alternative](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/Understanding/non-text-content.html#dfn-text-alternative) that serves the equivalent purpose, except for the situations listed below.
> [...]
> Decoration, Formatting, Invisible
**> If non-text content is [pure decoration](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/Understanding/non-text-content.html#dfn-pure-decoration), is used only for visual formatting, or is not presented to users, then it is implemented in a way that it can be ignored by [assistive technology](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/Understanding/non-text-content.html#dfn-assistive-technology).**
> 

The last paragraph says we must do what is needed to ensure that decorative elements are ignored, and the only way to do it, is to use `aria-hidden`. 

I'm going to make another PR about the tooltip icon's label (#40808 ) so that we can at least fix this label issue...

this approach seems a bit overengineered to me, but I can see why (since WCAG flip-flopped on whether or not preceding headings are or aren't programmatically determined context - see for instance https://github.com/w3c/wcag/issues/3361).

so, while a bit excessive, I don't mind this approach, but wondering: is this really the only place where this change would be needed? are there other stackblitz links anywhere else in the docs that would need this?

> Thoughts on linking to Collapse usage docs instead as mentioned?
> Eh, it's a similar situation to tooltips/popovers, so going to just roll with it.

As you mentioned, it's pretty much the same situation as with tooltips and popovers—the only real difference is that the word "tooltip" or "popover" is swapped out, and here it's just "collapse" instead. The content is nearly identical.

I don't mind the temporary duplication, though. We can always revisit this once the docs are migrated to Astro. It'll likely be easier to create reusable content there, especially since Hugo doesn’t handle nesting partials or shortcodes very well (at least from what I remember).

Unfortunately, according to our [`.browserslistrc`](https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/blob/main/.browserslistrc), we're still supposed to support Safari 12. Based on the data from https://caniuse.com/flexbox-gap only, flexbox gap is only supported starting from Safari 14, though I haven't manually verified this.

Unless I've overlooked something, we have two options here:
* We label this PR for v6, which aligns with our future update of [`.browserslistrc`](https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/blob/main/.browserslistrc)
* We close this PR and either mention this decision or cherry-pick your commit in [#37309](https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/pull/37309) for v6.
author: julien-deramond

body:
> Unfortunately, according to our [`.browserslistrc`](https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/blob/main/.browserslistrc?rgh-link-date=2024-09-18T01%3A44%3A45Z), we're still supposed to support Safari 12. Based on the data from [caniuse.com/flexbox-gap](https://caniuse.com/flexbox-gap) only, flexbox gap is only supported starting from Safari 14, though I haven't manually verified this.
> 
> Unless I've overlooked something, we have two options here:
> 
> * We label this PR for v6, which aligns with our future update of [`.browserslistrc`](https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/blob/main/.browserslistrc?rgh-link-date=2024-09-18T01%3A44%3A45Z)
> * We close this PR and either mention this decision or cherry-pick your commit in [#37309](https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/pull/37309) for v6.

I think rewording the callout could be a better option then. Perhaps something like 'Support for gap utilities with flexbox isn't available in Safari prior to 14.5, so consider verifying your intended browser support. Grid layout should have no issues. [Read more](https://caniuse.com/flexbox-gap).'

The main issue I have is that it currently says "...was **recently** added to Safari" which is no longer the case.

body:
Thanks for trying something for this issue @thet 
Unfortunately, Bootstrap v5 still need to support `node-sass` too. So using `sass:math` nor `sass:color` won't probably be possible. This is why this issue will be tricky IMO.

To test locally the `node-sass` compilation of Bootstrap: `npx --package node-sass@8 node-sass --output-style expanded --source-map true --source-map-contents true --precision 6 scss/ -o dist-sass/css/`

In this PR, the error is:

```
{
  "status": 1,
  "file": "/Users/ju/twbs/bootstrap/scss/_functions.scss",
  "line": 40,
  "column": 13,
  "message": "Invalid CSS after \"  $red: math\": expected expression (e.g. 1px, bold), was \".round(color.channe\"",
  "formatted": "Error: Invalid CSS after \"  $red: math\": expected expression (e.g. 1px, bold), was \".round(color.channe\"\n        on line 40 of scss/_functions.scss\n        from line 6 of scss/bootstrap-grid.scss\n>>   $red: math.round(color.channel($value, \"red\", rgb));\n\n   ------------^\n"
}
```

You can also ignore all major, minor, or patch releases for a dependency by adding an [`ignore` condition](https://docs.github.com/en/code-security/supply-chain-security/configuration-options-for-dependency-updates#ignore) with the desired `update_types` to your config file.

body:
Thanks for the PR @smitbhalodiya 
While I appreciate your contribution, we can't make this modification as it would introduce another inconsistency alongside other Sass variables like `$headings-font-style`, `$headings-font-weight`, and `$headings-line-height`. There are indeed several inconsistencies in naming conventions that need refactoring, but this would have to happen in a major version update, as it would be breaking for users. Unfortunately, we can't address it right now. I'll tag it for v6 to keep it in mind for the future :)
author: julien-deramond

I appreciate you tagging it for v6, and I'd be happy to contribute to the project in the future when the time is right for this kind of update.

Thanks again, and please feel free to reach out if there's anything else I can help with in the meantime!

Best regards,  
Smit Bhalodiya

author: fulldecent

body:
I can't speak for XhmikosR's decision to close this PR, but I can offer some arguments on why I would have raised at least concerns:
1. While support isn't an issue, as you noted, the original approach is probably more educational. It breaks the process into distinct steps: first, obtaining the trigger list, and then initializing the instances. This separation provides a clearer understanding of what's happening at each stage. It also allows more flexibility—for example, someone might want to target a specific element or subset of triggers later.
2. More importantly, this change would affect consistency across the documentation. The same initialization pattern is used in multiple places. If we were to update it, we'd need to ensure the change is applied everywhere for uniformity. Introducing different approaches within the same documentation could lead to confusion for users who rely on consistent examples.

I hope this helps :)

body:
Just to add some more context. This is not a bug in Bootstrap but in a css parser used in some pipelines. It was allegedly fixed in newer versions of the parser, but probably not backported, so it still affects some older projects. Updating the parser version might not be always possible given the spiderweb of dependencies.

Nevertheless as a very similar change was done in the past it'd make some sense to keep the code consistent, i.e. have `calc(negative_value * #{$variable})` in all places. That said, it also doesn't feel very natural to now have `calc(negative_value * #{$variable})` and `calc(#{$variable} * positive_value)` next to each other.  🤷

author: Manchmal1

body:
Sorry for the delay, @guilhas07, and thank you for taking the time to work on this PR!
I haven't digged into the history of the implementation of the Scrollspy component yet, nor looked at your code.
Here's my feedback after testing and comparing the behavior with the current implementation of the Scrollspy component.


- **General improvement:** Overall, this feels less buggy, as all items are active while scrolling. This is a great enhancement!
- **Behavior on specific pages:** On the [Reboot documentation page](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/content/reboot/), for example, the last element is active in the current implementation but not in your branch. Personally, I don’t mind this change—it aligns with how some other frameworks (e.g., Astro/Starlight) handle small sections that "stick" to the bottom of the page.
- In the [Scrollspy Navbar example from your branch](https://deploy-preview-41016--twbs-bootstrap.netlify.app/docs/5.3/components/scrollspy/#navbar), selecting an item from the dropdown and then scrolling results in two `.active` elements simultaneously (which shouldn't happen IMO).

![Screenshot 2024-12-18 at 08 53 13](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/ce2c20a1-1dbc-4f14-9113-688b205767f7)

- Possibly linked to one of my previous comments, in the [Scrollspy Nested Nav example from your branch](https://deploy-preview-41016--twbs-bootstrap.netlify.app/docs/5.3/components/scrollspy/#nested-nav), the last element (3.2) never becomes active, even when clicked.

![Screenshot 2024-12-18 at 08 55 30](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/66beb6da-8739-40d0-8b8f-641509a1daf9)

To wrap up, I think this PR has potential, but we need to address these edge cases. Once finalized, it would be great to add strict unit tests to enforce the new behavior. These tests could also act as documentation for the expected behavior, helping prevent regressions in the future.

**Edit**: be careful, the tests fail right now. You can run `npm run js-test` locally to replicate the issues.

Anyone interested in this topic, feel free to jump in and share your feedback! (/cc @twbs/js-review, you have more expertise on this than I do).

_(Note for us, maintainers: if this PR ends up being the main one to fix Scrollspy issues, let's add the references of all the issues related to the Scrollspy)_
author: julien-deramond

body:
First of all thank you for your feedback!

>General improvement: Overall, this feels less buggy, as all items are active while scrolling. This is a great enhancement!

Thank you!

>Behavior on specific pages: On the [Reboot documentation page](https://getbootstrap.com/docs/5.3/content/reboot/), for example, the last element is active in the current implementation but not in your branch. Personally, I don’t mind this change—it aligns with how some other frameworks (e.g., Astro/Starlight) handle small sections that "stick" to the bottom of the page.

I'm afraid as you already mentioned that is hard to do because technically there are two elements fully shown.

> In the [Scrollspy Navbar example from your branch](https://deploy-preview-41016--twbs-bootstrap.netlify.app/docs/5.3/components/scrollspy/#navbar), selecting an item from the dropdown and then scrolling results in two .active elements simultaneously (which shouldn't happen IMO).

Fixed.

> Possibly linked to one of my previous comments, in the [Scrollspy Nested Nav example from your branch](https://deploy-preview-41016--twbs-bootstrap.netlify.app/docs/5.3/components/scrollspy/#nested-nav), the last element (3.2) never becomes active, even when clicked.

Also fixed but this led me to change the current implementation.

>To wrap up, I think this PR has potential, but we need to address these edge cases. Once finalized, it would be great to add strict unit tests to enforce the new behavior. These tests could also act as documentation for the expected behavior, helping prevent regressions in the future.
Edit: be careful, the tests fail right now. You can run npm run js-test locally to replicate the issues.

I'm looking into it. I've modified some tests to align with the new behavior, and I will try to come up with new ones (if you have any suggestions, I'm all ears). ~~Currently, there is one test failing: `ScrollSpy constructor should not process data if the activeTarget is the same as the given target`. Do we need to keep this behavior?~~ Restored the behavior, so now every test is passing locally.

### Changes:

My previous implementation only considered `header` elements, which are small due to not wrapping the text in their section. However, when using entire div elements, it became impossible to activate the next element because, in many cases, two elements would be visible simultaneously. To address this, I now activate the element with the larger intersection ratio. To achieve this, I keep track of each element's intersection ratio and have increased the thresholds to recalculate the intersection ratios at every 10% increment.

The thing with dart sass and Hugo is that it requires extra steps from each user on their OS to install it.

For example, on my Windows VM, this branch right here won't work. Hence the use of hugo-bin. We should find a solution that works IMHO.

body:
only partially agree here. yes, `aria-current` should be on the links, not their parent list items.

however, it's the author's choice whether or not they want to have the current page's pagination as an *active* link or not. it's perfectly valid to decide not to make the current page's indicator a link at all. at that point, you can also omit the `aria-current` altogether.
author: patrickhlauke

body: Hello @patrickhlauke and @mdo, thank you for the replies :) Indeed it's the author's choice to put the pagination elements as link or not. The idea here is to provide an accessible example in Bootstrap that is aligned with major SRs (JAWS, NVDA) so that by default their code is a11y compliant.

body: Oh hmm, this does cause errors with `$form-select-focus-width` being unused. Unsure how I'd want to tackle yet.
author: mdo

body:
Oops... My fault, I just looked to the output of `npm run lint` without the necessary attention.

Removing both the unused variables (`$input-btn-focus-color` and `$form-select-focus-width`) seems to have no side effects.

More in general, I feel that all the `$input-btn-focus-*` variables are just aliases for `$focus-ring-*` and should be suppressed. `$btn-focus-*` already permit customization of buttons over inputs and can be defaulted directly to `$focus-ring-*`, like in a few other situations.

My proposal: fix the behavior of `select` with this PR (or a new, clean one, as I messed a bit around trying to loosely sync my branch...), and dive a bit deeper to remove some other redundant variable.

body: Yeah there are too many reused variables, so that's a thing I'm working on in v6. Need to reduce these things down a bit. As for this branch, we could mark those other variables as deprecated here and merge this, but I'd need @julien-deramond's thoughts on that.

body:
> As for this branch, we could mark those other variables as deprecated here and merge this

Hey! 
I’ve added [this commit](https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/pull/41284/commits/173acfd0646e04b70a5885ef3ae2feb1e4d857da) to retain the Sass variables and mark them as deprecated using fusv, following the approach we've used so far.

Since this would introduce breaking changes, we’ll need to hold off on merging the PR until v5.4.0 to avoid issues in a patch release.
I’ve moved this PR to the v5.4.0 project.
